  "text": "The Rust edition-based import path handling, prelude v1 vs v2 module exports, and resolver behavior changes between edition 2018 and 2021 span several key files:

## Standard Library Prelude Modules

### `library/core/src/prelude/mod.rs`
Defines all edition-specific prelude modules: `rust_2015`, `rust_2018`, `rust_2021`, `rust_2024`, and `rust_future`. The `rust_2015` and `rust_2018` modules simply re-export `super::v1::*`. The `rust_2021` module additionally adds `crate::iter::FromIterator` and `crate::convert::{TryFrom, TryInto}` — the key additions that changed behavior between 2018 and 2021.

### `library/core/src/prelude/v1.rs`
The base v1 prelude, re-exported by both `rust_2015` and `rust_2018`. Contains marker traits, ops, collections, and conversion traits but NOT `TryFrom`, `TryInto`, or `FromIterator`.

### `library/std/src/prelude/mod.rs`
The std-layer prelude: `rust_2015` and `rust_2018` re-export `super::v1::*`; `rust_2021` additionally re-exports `core::prelude::rust_2021::*` (which adds `TryFrom`, `TryInto`, `FromIterator`).

### `library/std/src/prelude/v1.rs`
The std base v1 prelude, including built-in macros and std-specific types re-exported from `core::prelude::v1`.

## Compiler: Injection of Edition-Specific Preludes

### `compiler/rustc_builtin_macros/src/standard_library_imports.rs`
The `inject()` function is the mechanism that inserts the edition-specific prelude import at the beginning of every crate. It:
- Emits `#[macro_use] extern crate std` (or `core`) with an `ident_span` that is the def-site span for edition >= 2018 (to make it hygienic), or the call-site span for 2015.
- Emits a `#[prelude_import] use ::std::prelude::rust_2015::*` (with absolute `::` root for 2015) or `use std::prelude::rust_2018::*` (no leading `::`) for 2018+.
- Selects the edition-appropriate module: `Edition2015 => sym::rust_2015`, `Edition2018 => sym::rust_2018`, `Edition2021 => sym::rust_2021`, etc.

## Compiler: Resolver Behavior Changes

### `compiler/rustc_resolve/src/lib.rs`
Defines the `macro_use_prelude` field (`FxIndexMap<Symbol, NameBinding>`) populated by `#[macro_use]` extern crates. Also defines `ModuleOrUniformRoot::ExternPrelude` (used for `::foo` paths in edition 2018+) and `ModuleAndExternPrelude` (for `::foo` from 2015 edition macros used in 2018+ crates).

### `compiler/rustc_resolve/src/ident.rs`
Core name resolution with edition-specific behavior:
- `kw::PathRoot` + `ident.span.at_least_rust_2018()` → resolves to `ExternPrelude` (2018+ behavior where `::foo` refers to external crates)
- `kw::PathRoot` + `ident.span.is_rust_2015()` + session is 2018+ → uses `ModuleAndExternPrelude` (cross-edition macro hygiene)
- `Scope::MacroUsePrelude` searches `macro_use_prelude` for macros imported via `#[macro_use]`
- Edition 2015 gets separate `rust_2015` flag controlling macro scope resolution

### `compiler/rustc_resolve/src/build_reduced_graph.rs`
Builds the reduced module graph. Handles `#[macro_use]` on extern crates (adding macros to `macro_use_prelude`). Enforces that visibility paths in 2018+ editions cannot be module-relative (only crate-relative via `crate::` or absolute `::`).

### `compiler/rustc_resolve/src/diagnostics.rs`
Edition-specific error messages: notes that `TryFrom`, `TryInto`, `FromIterator` are in the prelude starting Edition 2021, explains that `::foo` means extern prelude in 2018+, offers `crate::` prefix suggestions for 2018+.

### `compiler/rustc_resolve/src/macros.rs`
Macro name resolution, including scope ordering where prelude macros (from `macro_use_prelude` and std prelude) come after `macro_rules!` scopes.

## Compiler: Prelude Collision Lints

### `compiler/rustc_hir_typeck/src/method/prelude_edition_lints.rs`
Implements `lint_edition_dependent_dot_call()`, the check that warns when a method call (e.g., `.try_into()`) will become ambiguous in Rust 2021 because `TryInto` is added to the prelude. Fires `RUST_2021_PRELUDE_COLLISIONS` or `RUST_2024_PRELUDE_COLLISIONS`.

### `compiler/rustc_hir_typeck/src/method/suggest.rs`
Method resolution suggestions include a note about TryInto/TryFrom/FromIterator being added to the prelude in Edition 2021.

### `compiler/rustc_lint_defs/src/builtin.rs`
Declares `rust_2021_prelude_collisions` and `rust_2024_prelude_collisions` lints with edition-error future incompatibility references.

## Edition Abstraction

### `compiler/rustc_span/src/edition.rs`
The `Edition` enum with `is_rust_2015()`, `at_least_rust_2018()`, `at_least_rust_2021()`, `at_least_rust_2024()` methods. Used pervasively throughout the compiler to branch on edition-specific behavior."
}

{"content":"1: //! # The Rust Prelude 2: //! 3: //! Rust comes with a variety of things in its standard library. However, if 4: //! you had to manually import every single thing that you used, it would be 5: //! very verbose. But importing a lot of things that a program never uses isn't 6: //! good either. A balance needs to be struck. 7: //! 8: //! The *prelude* is the list of things that Rust automatically imports into 9: //! every Rust program. It's kept as small as possible, and is focused on 10: //! things, particularly traits, which are used in almost every single Rust 11: //! program. 12: //! 13: //! # Other preludes 14: //! 15: //! Preludes can be seen as a pattern to make using multiple types more 16: //! convenient. As such, you'll find other preludes in the standard library, 17: //! such as [`std::io::prelude`]. Various libraries in the Rust ecosystem may 18: //! also define their own preludes. 19: //! 20: //! [`std::io::prelude`]: crate::io::prelude 21: //! 22: //! The difference between 'the prelude' and these other preludes is that they 23: //! are not automatically `use`'d, and must be imported manually. This is still 24: //! easier than importing all of their constituent components. 25: //! 26: //! # Prelude contents 27: //! 28: //! The items included in the prelude depend on the edition of the crate. 29: //! The first version of the prelude is used in Rust 2015 and Rust 2018, 30: //! and lives in [`std::prelude::v1`]. 31: //! [`std::prelude::rust_2015`] and [`std::prelude::rust_2018`] re-export this prelude. 32: //! It re-exports the following: 33: //! 34: //! * [std::marker]::{[Copy], [Send], [Sized], [Sync], [Unpin]}, 35: //! marker traits that indicate fundamental properties of types. 36: //! * [std::ops]::{[Fn], [FnMut], [FnOnce]}, and their analogous 37: //! async traits, [std::ops]::{[AsyncFn], [AsyncFnMut], [AsyncFnOnce]}. 38: //! * [std::ops]::[Drop], for implementing destructors. 39: //! * [std::mem]::[drop], a convenience function for explicitly 40: //! dropping a value. 41: //! * [std::mem]::{[size_of], [size_of_val]}, to get the size of 42: //! a type or value. 43: //! * [std::mem]::{[align_of], [align_of_val]}, to get the 44: //! alignment of a type or value. 45: //! * [std::boxed]::[Box], a way to allocate values on the heap. 46: //! * [std::borrow]::[ToOwned], the conversion trait that defines 47: //! [`to_owned`], the generic method for creating an owned type from a 48: //! borrowed type. 49: //! * [std::clone]::[Clone], the ubiquitous trait that defines 50: //! [`clone`][Clone::clone], the method for producing a copy of a value. 51: //! * [std::cmp]::{[PartialEq], [PartialOrd], [Eq], [Ord]}, the 52: //! comparison traits, which implement the comparison operators and are often 53: //! seen in trait bounds. 54: //! * [std::convert]::{[AsRef], [AsMut], [Into], [From]}, generic 55: //! conversions, used by savvy API authors to create overloaded methods. 56: //! * [std::default]::[Default], types that have default values. 57: //! * [std::iter]::{[Iterator], [Extend], [IntoIterator], [DoubleEndedIterator], [ExactSizeIterator]}, 58: //! iterators of various 59: //! kinds. 60: //! * [std::option]::[Option]::{[self][Option], [Some], [None]}, a 61: //! type which expresses the presence or absence of a value. This type is so 62: //! commonly used, its variants are also exported. 63: //! * [std::result]::[Result]::{[self][Result], [Ok], [Err]}, a type 64: //! for functions that may succeed or fail. Like [`Option`], its variants are 65: //! exported as well. 66: //! * [std::string]::{[String], [ToString]}, heap-allocated strings. 67: //! * [std::vec]::[Vec], a growable, heap-allocated vector. 68: //! 69: //! The prelude used in Rust 2021, [`std::prelude::rust_2021`], includes all of the above, 70: //! and in addition re-exports: 71: //! 72: //! * [std::convert]::{[TryFrom], [TryInto]}. 73: //! * [std::iter]::[FromIterator]. 74: //! 75: //! The prelude used in Rust 2024, [`std::prelude::rust_2024`], includes all of the above, 76: //! and in addition re-exports: 77: //! 78: //! * [std::future]::{[Future], [IntoFuture]}. 79: //! 80: //! [std::borrow]: crate::borrow 81: //! [std::boxed]: crate::boxed 82: //! [std::clone]: crate::clone 83: //! [std::cmp]: crate::cmp 84: //! [std::convert]: crate::convert 85: //! [std::default]: crate::default 86: //! [std::future]: crate::future 87: //! [std::iter]: crate::iter 88: //! [std::marker]: crate::marker 89: //! [std::mem]: crate::mem 90: //! [`std::prelude::rust_2021`]: rust_2021 96: //! [`std::prelude::rust_2024`]: rust_2024 97: //! [std::result]: crate::result 98: //! [std::slice]: crate::slice 99: //! [std::string]: crate::string 100: //! [std::vec]: mod@crate::vec 101: //! [`to_owned`]: crate::borrow::ToOwned::to_owned 102: //! [book-closures]: ../../book/ch13-01-closures.html 103: //!   "text": "The Rust edition-based import path handling, prelude v1 vs v2 module exports, and resolver behavior changes between edition 2018 and 2021 span several key files:

## Standard Library Prelude Modules

### `library/core/src/prelude/mod.rs`
Defines all edition-specific prelude modules: `rust_2015`, `rust_2018`, `rust_2021`, `rust_2024`, and `rust_future`. The `rust_2015` and `rust_2018` modules simply re-export `super::v1::*`. The `rust_2021` module additionally adds `crate::iter::FromIterator` and `crate::convert::{TryFrom, TryInto}` — the key additions that changed behavior between 2018 and 2021.

### `library/core/src/prelude/v1.rs`
The base v1 prelude, re-exported by both `rust_2015` and `rust_2018`. Contains marker traits, ops, collections, and conversion traits but NOT `TryFrom`, `TryInto`, or `FromIterator`.

### `library/std/src/prelude/mod.rs`
The std-layer prelude: `rust_2015` and `rust_2018` re-export `super::v1::*`; `rust_2021` additionally re-exports `core::prelude::rust_2021::*` (which adds `TryFrom`, `TryInto`, `FromIterator`).

### `library/std/src/prelude/v1.rs`
The std base v1 prelude, including built-in macros and std-specific types re-exported from `core::prelude::v1`.

## Compiler: Injection of Edition-Specific Preludes

### `compiler/rustc_builtin_macros/src/standard_library_imports.rs`
The `inject()` function is the mechanism that inserts the edition-specific prelude import at the beginning of every crate. It:
- Emits `#[macro_use] extern crate std` (or `core`) with an `ident_span` that is the def-site span for edition >= 2018 (to make it hygienic), or the call-site span for 2015.
- Emits a `#[prelude_import] use ::std::prelude::rust_2015::*` (with absolute `::` root for 2015) or `use std::prelude::rust_2018::*` (no leading `::`) for 2018+.
- Selects the edition-appropriate module: `Edition2015 => sym::rust_2015`, `Edition2018 => sym::rust_2018`, `Edition2021 => sym::rust_2021`, etc.

## Compiler: Resolver Behavior Changes

### `compiler/rustc_resolve/src/lib.rs`
Defines the `macro_use_prelude` field (`FxIndexMap<Symbol, NameBinding>`) populated by `#[macro_use]` extern crates. Also defines `ModuleOrUniformRoot::ExternPrelude` (used for `::foo` paths in edition 2018+) and `ModuleAndExternPrelude` (for `::foo` from 2015 edition macros used in 2018+ crates).

### `compiler/rustc_resolve/src/ident.rs`
Core name resolution with edition-specific behavior:
- `kw::PathRoot` + `ident.span.at_least_rust_2018()` → resolves to `ExternPrelude` (2018+ behavior where `::foo` refers to external crates)
- `kw::PathRoot` + `ident.span.is_rust_2015()` + session is 2018+ → uses `ModuleAndExternPrelude` (cross-edition macro hygiene)
- `Scope::MacroUsePrelude` searches `macro_use_prelude` for macros imported via `#[macro_use]`
- Edition 2015 gets separate `rust_2015` flag controlling macro scope resolution

### `compiler/rustc_resolve/src/build_reduced_graph.rs`
Builds the reduced module graph. Handles `#[macro_use]` on extern crates (adding macros to `macro_use_prelude`). Enforces that visibility paths in 2018+ editions cannot be module-relative (only crate-relative via `crate::` or absolute `::`).

### `compiler/rustc_resolve/src/diagnostics.rs`
Edition-specific error messages: notes that `TryFrom`, `TryInto`, `FromIterator` are in the prelude starting Edition 2021, explains that `::foo` means extern prelude in 2018+, offers `crate::` prefix suggestions for 2018+.

### `compiler/rustc_resolve/src/macros.rs`
Macro name resolution, including scope ordering where prelude macros (from `macro_use_prelude` and std prelude) come after `macro_rules!` scopes.

## Compiler: Prelude Collision Lints

### `compiler/rustc_hir_typeck/src/method/prelude_edition_lints.rs`
Implements `lint_edition_dependent_dot_call()`, the check that warns when a method call (e.g., `.try_into()`) will become ambiguous in Rust 2021 because `TryInto` is added to the prelude. Fires `RUST_2021_PRELUDE_COLLISIONS` or `RUST_2024_PRELUDE_COLLISIONS`.

### `compiler/rustc_hir_typeck/src/method/suggest.rs`
Method resolution suggestions include a note about TryInto/TryFrom/FromIterator being added to the prelude in Edition 2021.

### `compiler/rustc_lint_defs/src/builtin.rs`
Declares `rust_2021_prelude_collisions` and `rust_2024_prelude_collisions` lints with edition-error future incompatibility references.

## Edition Abstraction

### `compiler/rustc_span/src/edition.rs`
The `Edition` enum with `is_rust_2015()`, `at_least_rust_2018()`, `at_least_rust_2021()`, `at_least_rust_2024()` methods. Used pervasively throughout the compiler to branch on edition-specific behavior."
